---
title: "Modes of Convergence: In Probability and In Distribution"
subtitle: "Core Sample · Probability Foundations"
---

```{python}
#| label: setup
#| include: false
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# A consistent, restrained visual style for every figure in this article.
plt.rcParams.update({
    "figure.figsize": (6.2, 4.0),
    "figure.dpi": 140,
    "axes.spines.top": False,
    "axes.spines.right": False,
    "axes.grid": True,
    "grid.alpha": 0.25,
    "font.size": 11,
    "axes.titlesize": 12,
    "lines.linewidth": 2.0,
})

rng = np.random.default_rng(20210)

SEQ    = "#2b3a67"     # the sequence X_n
LIMIT  = "#c1121f"     # the limit X (or its law)
ACCENT = "#0a7d4d"     # highlights / exact values
GREY   = "#6c757d"
```

> **Where this sits in the track.** This is the article that closes Movement 4 of the
> Probability Foundations track — the place where the two pieces of tail-and-limit
> machinery finally have something to converge *to*. It assumes [PROB-05 *Expectation*]
> (the expectation operator), [PROB-06 *Variance*] (the second moment), and [PROB-08
> *Independence and the i.i.d. Sample*] (so that "the sample mean of $n$ i.i.d. draws" is
> a defined object). Its two direct parents are the siblings that feed it:
> [PROB-19 *Chebyshev's Inequality and the Concentration Idea*], whose
> $P(|X-\mu|\ge k\sigma)\le 1/k^2$ is the engine that *produces* convergence in
> probability, and [PROB-20 *Moment Generating and Characteristic Functions*], whose
> characteristic function $\varphi_X(t)=E[e^{itX}]$ is the tool that *detects* convergence
> in distribution. We assume nothing above the Calc-1 floor: every $\varepsilon$–$N$ limit
> argument is written out, and where we need a fact about characteristic functions it is
> imported by name from [PROB-20], never silently. It **sets up** the entire asymptotic
> half of [INFR-A2] (the law of large numbers and the central limit theorem are *stated*
> in the modes defined here), [INFR-B4] (asymptotic normality of the maximum-likelihood
> estimator), and the **delta method**. The clean ownership seam the track is built around:
> *we own the convergence vocabulary and its hierarchy; Inference owns the theorems that
> deliver the limits.*

## Abstract {.unnumbered}

A single random variable is a number-with-uncertainty. But the deep results of statistics
are about *sequences* — $\bar X_n$ as the sample size $n$ grows, an estimator $\hat\theta_n$
as data accumulates — and a sequence of random variables can "approach" a limit in more
than one sense, because there are at least two different things one might want to hold
steady: the *values* the variables take, or the *distribution* those values are drawn from.
This article defines the modes we actually use — **convergence in probability** (the values
cluster near a target with overwhelming probability) and **convergence in distribution**
(the entire law settles onto a limiting law), with **almost-sure** and **$L^2$**
convergence built alongside for contrast. It then proves the **hierarchy** that organizes
them — almost sure $\Rightarrow$ in probability $\Rightarrow$ in distribution — derives each
implication step by step, and shows by explicit counterexample that none of the arrows
reverses. Finally it builds the two combination rules every applied asymptotic argument
runs on: the **continuous mapping theorem** and **Slutsky's theorem**. The single claim
this article defends is that **these are not four interchangeable notions of "the sequence
settles down" but a strictly ordered ladder**, and that knowing exactly which rung a result
lives on is what lets you combine limits without proving something false.

# The Core {#sec-core}

::: {.callout-important title="The Core"}
A sequence of random variables $X_1, X_2, \dots$ can approach a limit in several
inequivalent senses, ordered from strongest to weakest:

- **Almost surely** ($X_n \xrightarrow{a.s.} X$): the *realized path* $X_n(\omega)$
  converges to $X(\omega)$ as an ordinary sequence of numbers, for every outcome $\omega$
  except a set of probability zero.
- **In probability** ($X_n \xrightarrow{P} X$): for every tolerance $\varepsilon>0$, the
  probability that $X_n$ is more than $\varepsilon$ away from $X$ shrinks to zero,
  $P(|X_n - X| > \varepsilon)\to 0$.
- **In distribution** ($X_n \xrightarrow{d} X$): the *cumulative distribution functions*
  converge, $F_{X_n}(x)\to F_X(x)$ at every $x$ where $F_X$ is continuous — the law settles,
  even if the variables themselves never get close to any single number.

These satisfy a one-way hierarchy
$$
\text{a.s.} \;\Longrightarrow\; \text{in probability} \;\Longrightarrow\; \text{in distribution},
$$
and **no arrow reverses** in general. The law of large numbers delivers the middle rung
(the sample mean converges *in probability* to the true mean); the central limit theorem
delivers the bottom rung (the standardized sample mean converges *in distribution* to a
normal). Two glue results — the **continuous mapping theorem** ($X_n\to X$ survives a
continuous function $g$) and **Slutsky's theorem** (a convergent-in-distribution sequence
combines cleanly with a convergent-in-probability one) — let us assemble these limits into
real statistical statements.
:::

Everything below orbits that box. The reason there is *more than one* mode is the single
most important idea here, and it is easy to miss if you have only ever taken limits of
ordinary numbers. With numbers, "the sequence approaches $L$" means one thing. With random
variables there are two layers — the outcome $\omega$ that nature draws, and the number
$X_n(\omega)$ that the draw produces — and "settling down" can mean *the numbers settle for
each path* or merely *the histogram of numbers settles*. Those are genuinely different, and
the entire article is about pinning down how different and why it matters.

# Why we are here: a limit problem with no single answer {#sec-why}

Keep one question in view for the whole article: **when we say a sequence of random
variables "converges," which of several possible things do we mean — and which one does a
given theorem actually give us?** Every definition and every counterexample below is in
service of answering that precisely.

## The problem the modes were invented to solve {#sec-why-problem}

Almost everything statistics promises is a statement about $n\to\infty$. "Collect more data
and your estimate gets better." "The sample mean is approximately normal for large samples."
"The bootstrap distribution approaches the sampling distribution." Each is a claim that some
sequence of random quantities — $\bar X_n$, $\hat\theta_n$, an empirical CDF $\hat F_n$ —
*converges*. But a random variable is not a number; it is a function on the sample space.
So "$X_n$ converges to $X$" is not yet a defined phrase. We have to *say what we mean*, and
the historical discovery — the thing that took probabilists from Jacob Bernoulli in 1713 to
the modern definitions — is that there is **no single right meaning**. Different theorems
naturally produce different strengths of convergence, and conflating them produces false
proofs.

Here is the tension in its sharpest form. Consider two true facts about the sample mean
$\bar X_n = \frac1n\sum_{i=1}^n X_i$ of i.i.d. draws with mean $\mu$ and variance $\sigma^2$:

1. **The law of large numbers.** $\bar X_n$ gets close to $\mu$ and stays close — the
   *value* of the average homes in on the constant $\mu$.
2. **The central limit theorem.** The *rescaled* average $\sqrt n\,(\bar X_n - \mu)$ does
   **not** settle to any number; it keeps fluctuating forever, but its *distribution*
   settles onto a fixed normal $\mathcal N(0,\sigma^2)$.

Both are convergence statements about sequences built from the same data, yet they are
incompatible if "converges" means only one thing — the first says the sequence pins to a
point, the second says a closely related sequence never pins to a point but its *shape*
freezes. The resolution is that they are statements in **different modes**: the LLN is
convergence *in probability* (and, in its strong form, almost surely), and the CLT is
convergence *in distribution*. The modes exist precisely so that both of these true
statements can be stated without contradiction. That is the *why*, and we will hold onto it:
each mode earns its place by being exactly the kind of convergence some real theorem
produces.

## A picture of the two kinds of "settling down" {#sec-why-picture}

Before any $\varepsilon$, look at what the two phenomena look like. @fig-two-modes shows the
two stories side by side for i.i.d. draws from a distribution with mean $\mu=0$.

```{python}
#| label: fig-two-modes
#| fig-cap: 'Two senses of convergence for the same i.i.d. data (Exponential(1) centered to mean 0). Left: the running sample mean $\\bar X_n$ collapses onto the constant $\\mu=0$ — its *value* settles (convergence in probability / a.s.). Right: the standardized average $\\sqrt n\\,\\bar X_n$ never settles to a number — each panel''s histogram is a fresh batch of its fluctuations — but its *distribution* freezes onto a normal (convergence in distribution). Same data, two genuinely different limits.'

fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(9.2, 4.0))

# LEFT: many trajectories of the running mean collapsing to 0.
mu = 1.0  # Exponential(1) has mean 1; we center by subtracting mu.
n_max = 400
for _ in range(40):
    draws = rng.exponential(scale=1.0, size=n_max) - mu
    running_mean = np.cumsum(draws) / np.arange(1, n_max + 1)
    axL.plot(np.arange(1, n_max + 1), running_mean, color=SEQ, alpha=0.18)
axL.axhline(0.0, color=LIMIT, lw=2.2, label=r"$\mu = 0$ (the limit)")
axL.set_xlabel(r"sample size $n$")
axL.set_ylabel(r"$\bar X_n$")
axL.set_title("Convergence in probability:\nthe value collapses")
axL.set_ylim(-1.2, 1.2)
axL.legend(loc="upper right", frameon=False)

# RIGHT: distribution of sqrt(n) * mean for growing n freezes onto a normal.
sigma = 1.0  # Exponential(1) has variance 1.
xs = np.linspace(-4, 4, 300)
normal_pdf = np.exp(-xs**2 / (2 * sigma**2)) / np.sqrt(2 * np.pi * sigma**2)
for n, shade in [(2, 0.25), (8, 0.5), (50, 0.85)]:
    samples = rng.exponential(scale=1.0, size=(8000, n)) - mu
    stat = np.sqrt(n) * samples.mean(axis=1)
    axR.hist(stat, bins=60, density=True, histtype="step",
             color=SEQ, alpha=shade, label=fr"$n={n}$")
axR.plot(xs, normal_pdf, color=LIMIT, lw=2.2, label=r"$\mathcal N(0,1)$ limit")
axR.set_xlabel(r"$\sqrt n\,(\bar X_n - \mu)$")
axR.set_ylabel("density")
axR.set_title("Convergence in distribution:\nthe shape freezes")
axR.set_xlim(-4, 4)
axR.legend(loc="upper right", frameon=False, fontsize=9)

fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

The left panel is convergence **of values**: trace any one path and it visibly funnels onto
the line $\mu=0$. The right panel is convergence **of shape**: no single path settles —
$\sqrt n\,\bar X_n$ has standard deviation $\sigma$ for *every* $n$, so it never stops
moving — yet the *histogram* of where it lands stabilizes into the bell curve. Hold both
pictures in mind; the definitions below are just these two pictures made precise, plus two
stronger notions that refine the left panel.

# The formal development {#sec-formal}

We now make each mode precise. Throughout, $X_1, X_2, \dots$ is a sequence of random
variables and $X$ is a "target" random variable (often a constant), all defined on the same
probability space — meaning they are functions $X_n:\Omega\to\mathbb R$ of the same
underlying outcome $\omega$, the object built in [PROB-02]. We define the modes from
strongest to weakest, then prove the chain that connects them.

## Mode 1 — Convergence in probability {#sec-in-prob}

This is the mode the law of large numbers lives in, and the one Chebyshev hands us directly.

::: {#def-in-prob}
**Convergence in probability.** The sequence $X_n$ **converges in probability** to $X$,
written $X_n \xrightarrow{P} X$, if for every $\varepsilon>0$,
$$
\lim_{n\to\infty} P\bigl(|X_n - X| > \varepsilon\bigr) = 0 .
$$ {#eq-def-in-prob}
Equivalently, $P(|X_n - X| \le \varepsilon)\to 1$. In words: fix any tolerance band of
half-width $\varepsilon$ around $X$; the probability that $X_n$ falls *outside* the band can
be driven below any threshold by taking $n$ large enough.
:::

Two things deserve to be unpacked, because the definition is doing something subtler than it
looks. First, $\varepsilon$ is the *width of the band* and is fixed before $n$ grows; the
quantifier order is "for each $\varepsilon$, the probability $\to 0$," not "the probability
$\to 0$ uniformly in $\varepsilon$." Second — and this is the crucial point that
distinguishes this mode from the stronger ones below — the event $\{|X_n - X|>\varepsilon\}$
is allowed to be a *different* set of outcomes for each $n$. Convergence in probability says
the *size* of the bad set shrinks; it says nothing about whether the *same* outcome
$\omega$ keeps being bad. An $\omega$ can be inside the band at step $n$, outside at step
$n+1$, inside again at $n+2$, forever — as long as the total probability of being-outside
decays. This is precisely the loophole that almost-sure convergence (below) closes, and the
gap between them is the most important conceptual fork in the article.

### Chebyshev gives convergence in probability for free {#sec-chebyshev-gives}

Here is the payoff of the sibling article, made explicit so nothing is deferred. Suppose the
$X_n$ have means $E[X_n]=\mu$ and variances $\operatorname{Var}(X_n)=v_n$ with $v_n\to 0$.
Then $X_n \xrightarrow{P}\mu$. The proof is one line of Chebyshev's inequality, which we
restate from [PROB-19] as the imported ingredient: for any random variable $Y$ with mean
$E[Y]$ and finite variance, and any $a>0$,
$$
P\bigl(|Y - E[Y]| \ge a\bigr) \;\le\; \frac{\operatorname{Var}(Y)}{a^2} .
$$ {#eq-chebyshev}
This is *the one fact we borrow*; its derivation (Markov's inequality applied to $(Y-E[Y])^2$)
is owned by [PROB-19] and not repeated here. Apply it with $Y = X_n$ and $a=\varepsilon$:
$$
P\bigl(|X_n - \mu| \ge \varepsilon\bigr) \;\le\; \frac{v_n}{\varepsilon^2} .
$$
Now hold $\varepsilon$ fixed. The right-hand side is $v_n/\varepsilon^2$, a constant
$1/\varepsilon^2$ times $v_n$, and $v_n\to 0$ by hypothesis, so the right-hand side tends to
$0$. A probability is squeezed between $0$ and that vanishing bound, so by the squeeze
$P(|X_n-\mu|\ge\varepsilon)\to 0$. Since $\varepsilon>0$ was arbitrary, @eq-def-in-prob holds
with $X=\mu$. That is the whole argument, every step shown.

The headline instance is the **weak law of large numbers**, which [PROB-19] built to the
"sketch" level and [INFR-A2] owns in full. For i.i.d. $X_i$ with mean $\mu$ and finite
variance $\sigma^2$, the sample mean $\bar X_n=\frac1n\sum_{i=1}^n X_i$ has — by linearity
of expectation ([PROB-05]) and the variance-of-an-independent-sum rule ([PROB-08]) —
$$
E[\bar X_n]=\mu, \qquad
\operatorname{Var}(\bar X_n)=\frac{1}{n^2}\sum_{i=1}^n \operatorname{Var}(X_i)
=\frac{n\sigma^2}{n^2}=\frac{\sigma^2}{n}\xrightarrow[n\to\infty]{}0 .
$$ {#eq-mean-var}
The variance vanishes, so the result just proved gives $\bar X_n\xrightarrow{P}\mu$
immediately. The reason the modes were invented (@sec-why-problem) is now concrete: the LLN
*is* a convergence-in-probability statement, and Chebyshev is exactly the tool that produces
it.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
**"What's the difference between $X_n\to\mu$ in probability and saying the variance goes to
zero?"** They are closely related but not identical, and the precise relationship is worth
having ready. Vanishing variance *with a fixed mean $\mu$* is **sufficient** for convergence
in probability to $\mu$ — that is exactly the Chebyshev argument above. But it is **not
necessary**: a sequence can converge in probability while having infinite or undefined
variance (Chebyshev is one route to the conclusion, not the only one). And vanishing
variance alone, without controlling the mean, says nothing — if $E[X_n]=n$ then $X_n$ marches
off to infinity no matter how tight its spread. The clean statement: **bias plus spread**.
Write $X_n-\mu = (X_n-E[X_n]) + (E[X_n]-\mu)$; if both the standard deviation
$\sqrt{\operatorname{Var}(X_n)}$ and the bias $E[X_n]-\mu$ go to zero, then $X_n\xrightarrow{P}\mu$.
This is the "consistency" condition an interviewer is really probing — convergence in
probability to the truth is what *consistency of an estimator* means.
:::

## Mode 2 — Almost-sure convergence {#sec-as}

This is the strongest mode in the article, and it closes the loophole flagged above.

::: {#def-as}
**Almost-sure convergence.** The sequence $X_n$ **converges almost surely** to $X$, written
$X_n \xrightarrow{a.s.} X$, if
$$
P\Bigl(\bigl\{\omega : \lim_{n\to\infty} X_n(\omega) = X(\omega)\bigr\}\Bigr) = 1 .
$$ {#eq-def-as}
In words: collect all the outcomes $\omega$ for which the ordinary numerical sequence
$X_1(\omega), X_2(\omega), \dots$ converges to $X(\omega)$ in the Calc-1 sense; that set has
probability $1$. The exceptional outcomes where the path fails to converge form a set of
probability $0$.
:::

The contrast with @def-in-prob is the whole point. Almost-sure convergence is a statement
about the **realized path**: pick the outcome $\omega$ first, then look at the entire
trajectory $n\mapsto X_n(\omega)$, and demand that *this one numerical sequence* converges in
the elementary sense — for every $\varepsilon$ there is an $N$ (which may depend on $\omega$)
beyond which the whole tail of the path stays within $\varepsilon$ of the target. Convergence
in probability never makes a statement about a whole path; it only controls the probability
of the bad set *at each fixed $n$*. The "in probability" loophole — an outcome can be bad
infinitely often as long as the total probability of bad-ness decays — is exactly what
"almost surely" forbids: under a.s. convergence, for almost every $\omega$ the path is bad
only *finitely* often. The **strong law of large numbers** (owned by [INFR-A2]) is the
statement that $\bar X_n\xrightarrow{a.s.}\mu$; it upgrades the weak law's "in probability"
to "almost surely."

We also record, for the hierarchy, one more mode that sits *above* convergence in
probability but is not comparable to almost-sure convergence — it is the mode the variance
argument most naturally produces.

::: {#def-l2}
**Convergence in $L^2$ (mean square).** The sequence $X_n$ **converges in $L^2$** (or *in
mean square*) to $X$, written $X_n \xrightarrow{L^2} X$, if each $X_n$ and $X$ have finite
second moment and
$$
\lim_{n\to\infty} E\bigl[(X_n - X)^2\bigr] = 0 .
$$ {#eq-def-l2}
The quantity $E[(X_n-X)^2]$ is the **mean squared error** between $X_n$ and $X$; $L^2$
convergence says that error vanishes. ($L^2$ is named for the square; the general $L^p$
replaces the exponent $2$ by $p$, but $p=2$ is the one statistics lives in.)
:::

These four modes — a.s., $L^2$, in probability, in distribution — are all the "settling down"
notions we will use. Almost-sure and $L^2$ both sit strictly above in-probability; neither
implies the other (a fact we will not belabor with a counterexample, since the two arrows we
*do* need for the track are the ones in the Core box). The next two subsections prove the
spine: a.s. $\Rightarrow$ in probability $\Rightarrow$ in distribution.

## Mode 3 — Convergence in distribution {#sec-in-dist}

This is the weakest mode, and the one the central limit theorem lives in.

::: {#def-in-dist}
**Convergence in distribution.** The sequence $X_n$ **converges in distribution** (or *in
law*, or *weakly*) to $X$, written $X_n \xrightarrow{d} X$, if
$$
\lim_{n\to\infty} F_{X_n}(x) = F_X(x)
\qquad\text{for every } x \text{ at which } F_X \text{ is continuous},
$$ {#eq-def-in-dist}
where $F_{X_n}(x)=P(X_n\le x)$ and $F_X(x)=P(X\le x)$ are the cumulative distribution
functions from [PROB-02]. In words: the CDFs converge pointwise, except possibly at the jump
points of the limit CDF.
:::

Two features of this definition are non-negotiable and an interviewer will test both. First,
it is a statement about **distributions, not variables**: the $X_n$ need not be close to $X$
as functions of $\omega$ — indeed they need not live on the same probability space at all,
and $X$ can be a brand-new variable with no relation to the $X_n$ beyond having the limiting
law. (Contrast @def-in-prob and @def-as, which both require $|X_n-X|$ to be a meaningful,
same-space quantity.) Second, the **"continuity points" caveat is essential, not pedantic**.
Here is the canonical reason it is there, derived rather than asserted.

::: {#thm-continuity-caveat}
**Why convergence in distribution must exempt the jump points.** Let $X_n$ be the constant
$1/n$ (a degenerate random variable putting all its mass at $1/n$), and let $X$ be the
constant $0$. Then $X_n\xrightarrow{d} X$ — yet $F_{X_n}(0)\not\to F_X(0)$.
:::

::: {.proof}
The CDF of the constant $c$ is the step $F(x)=\mathbf 1\{x\ge c\}$ — it is $0$ for $x<c$ and
$1$ for $x\ge c$, because $P(c\le x)$ is $1$ exactly when $x$ has reached $c$. So
$$
F_{X_n}(x)=\mathbf 1\{x\ge 1/n\}, \qquad F_X(x)=\mathbf 1\{x\ge 0\}.
$$
Take any fixed $x$ and let $n\to\infty$.

- If $x<0$: then $x<1/n$ for all $n$, so $F_{X_n}(x)=0$ for all $n$, and $F_X(x)=0$. They
  agree.
- If $x>0$: then for all $n$ large enough that $1/n<x$ (i.e. $n>1/x$), we have $x\ge 1/n$, so
  $F_{X_n}(x)=1$ from that point on; and $F_X(x)=1$. They agree in the limit.
- If $x=0$: here $F_X(0)=\mathbf 1\{0\ge 0\}=1$. But $0\ge 1/n$ is **false** for every
  finite $n$ (since $1/n>0$), so $F_{X_n}(0)=0$ for all $n$, and the limit is $0\ne 1=F_X(0)$.

So the CDFs converge at every $x\ne 0$ and *fail* to converge only at $x=0$, which is exactly
the single discontinuity (jump point) of $F_X$. Morally, $1/n$ certainly "converges to $0$,"
so any sensible definition must call this convergence in distribution; the only way to
rescue that is to **excuse the limit's jump points** — which is precisely what @def-in-dist
does. The caveat is forced on us by examples like this one, not bolted on for safety.
:::

@fig-cdf-converge shows this pointwise-CDF picture for a non-degenerate case — the CLT
example from @sec-why-picture — so you can see @eq-def-in-dist literally happening.

```{python}
#| label: fig-cdf-converge
#| fig-cap: 'Convergence in distribution as pointwise convergence of CDFs. The empirical CDF of $\\sqrt n\\,(\\bar X_n-\\mu)$ (built from many simulated samples, Exponential(1) summands) is plotted for growing $n$; it climbs toward the standard-normal CDF (red). Convergence in distribution is exactly this curve-onto-curve approach — no claim that any individual value settles.'

xs = np.linspace(-4, 4, 400)
# Standard normal CDF via the error function relation, computed by numeric integration
# of the pdf (Calc-1 trapezoid) to avoid importing scipy.
grid = np.linspace(-12, 4, 4000)
pdf = np.exp(-grid**2 / 2) / np.sqrt(2 * np.pi)
cdf_vals = np.array([np.trapezoid(pdf[grid <= x], grid[grid <= x]) if np.any(grid <= x) else 0.0
                     for x in xs])

fig, ax = plt.subplots()
for n, shade in [(1, 0.30), (3, 0.55), (30, 0.9)]:
    samples = rng.exponential(scale=1.0, size=(20000, n)) - 1.0
    stat = np.sqrt(n) * samples.mean(axis=1)
    stat_sorted = np.sort(stat)
    ecdf = np.arange(1, len(stat_sorted) + 1) / len(stat_sorted)
    ax.plot(stat_sorted, ecdf, color=SEQ, alpha=shade, label=fr"$F_{{X_n}}$, $n={n}$")
ax.plot(xs, cdf_vals, color=LIMIT, lw=2.4, label=r"$\Phi$ (standard normal CDF)")
ax.set_xlabel(r"$x$")
ax.set_ylabel(r"$F_{X_n}(x) = P(X_n \le x)$")
ax.set_xlim(-4, 4)
ax.set_ylim(-0.02, 1.02)
ax.set_title("CDFs converging pointwise to the limit CDF")
ax.legend(loc="upper left", frameon=False, fontsize=9)
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

### The characteristic-function handle {#sec-cf-handle}

Checking @eq-def-in-dist directly — chasing CDFs to a limit — is usually hopeless, just as
the convolution integral was hopeless in [PROB-20]. The tool that rescues it is the one that
article built. We import it as a named ingredient (its proof, Lévy's continuity theorem, is
owned by [PROB-20] and used in the CLT by [INFR-A2]):

::: {#thm-levy}
**Lévy's continuity theorem (imported from [PROB-20]).** Let $\varphi_{X_n}(t)=E[e^{itX_n}]$
be the characteristic function of $X_n$, and $\varphi_X(t)=E[e^{itX}]$ that of $X$. Then
$$
X_n \xrightarrow{d} X
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\varphi_{X_n}(t)\to\varphi_X(t)\ \text{ for every } t\in\mathbb R .
$$
:::

This is *the* reason convergence in distribution is tractable: it turns "CDFs converge" into
"a single function of $t$ converges," and characteristic functions of sums multiply
([PROB-20]). It is exactly the machine that made the $\sqrt n\,\bar X_n \to \mathcal N(0,1)$
limit a one-line calculation in [PROB-20]'s CLT sketch, and it is the bottom rung's analogue
of Chebyshev for the middle rung. We will lean on it again for the continuous mapping theorem.

## The hierarchy {#sec-hierarchy}

Now the spine of the article: the two implications in the Core box, each proved, and a
counterexample showing each is one-directional.

### Almost sure $\Rightarrow$ in probability {#sec-as-implies-p}

::: {#thm-as-to-p}
If $X_n \xrightarrow{a.s.} X$ then $X_n \xrightarrow{P} X$.
:::

::: {.proof}
Fix $\varepsilon>0$; we must show $P(|X_n-X|>\varepsilon)\to 0$. Define, for each $n$, the
"tail bad set"
$$
B_n = \{\omega : |X_m(\omega)-X(\omega)|>\varepsilon \text{ for some } m\ge n\}
    = \bigcup_{m\ge n}\{|X_m - X|>\varepsilon\}.
$$
The sets $B_n$ are **nested and shrinking**: $B_{n+1}\subseteq B_n$, because the union
defining $B_{n+1}$ is over a smaller index set ($m\ge n+1$ instead of $m\ge n$). So they
decrease toward their intersection $B_\infty=\bigcap_n B_n$, and by continuity of probability
from above — the elementary axiom-consequence proved in [PROB-00], that for a decreasing
sequence of events $P(B_n)\to P(B_\infty)$ — we have
$$
\lim_{n\to\infty} P(B_n) = P(B_\infty).
$$
Now identify $B_\infty$. An outcome $\omega$ lies in $B_\infty$ iff it lies in *every* $B_n$,
i.e. iff for *every* $n$ there is some $m\ge n$ with $|X_m(\omega)-X(\omega)|>\varepsilon$ —
in other words, iff $|X_m(\omega)-X(\omega)|>\varepsilon$ happens for **infinitely many** $m$.
But if the path $X_m(\omega)$ converges to $X(\omega)$ as ordinary numbers, then by the
Calc-1 definition of a limit, for *this* $\varepsilon$ there is an $N$ beyond which
$|X_m(\omega)-X(\omega)|\le\varepsilon$ for *all* $m\ge N$ — so the deviation exceeds
$\varepsilon$ only finitely often, and $\omega\notin B_\infty$. Contrapositively, every
$\omega\in B_\infty$ is an outcome where the path **fails** to converge to $X$. By the
hypothesis $X_n\xrightarrow{a.s.}X$ (@eq-def-as), the set of non-convergent outcomes has
probability $0$, and $B_\infty$ is contained in it, so $P(B_\infty)=0$. Therefore
$P(B_n)\to 0$.

Finally, the event we actually care about is contained in the tail bad set:
$\{|X_n-X|>\varepsilon\}\subseteq B_n$ (take $m=n$ in the union). Monotonicity of probability
gives $P(|X_n-X|>\varepsilon)\le P(B_n)\to 0$. Since $\varepsilon$ was arbitrary,
$X_n\xrightarrow{P}X$.
:::

### In probability $\Rightarrow$ in distribution {#sec-p-implies-d}

::: {#thm-p-to-d}
If $X_n \xrightarrow{P} X$ then $X_n \xrightarrow{d} X$.
:::

::: {.proof}
Fix a point $x$ at which $F_X$ is continuous; we must show $F_{X_n}(x)\to F_X(x)$, i.e.
$P(X_n\le x)\to P(X\le x)$. The idea is a **sandwich**: relate the event $\{X_n\le x\}$ to
the events $\{X\le x-\varepsilon\}$ and $\{X\le x+\varepsilon\}$, using that $X_n$ is close to
$X$ with high probability.

*Upper relation.* Whenever $X_n\le x$, either $X\le x+\varepsilon$ holds, or it fails — and
if it fails then $X>x+\varepsilon$ while $X_n\le x$, which forces
$|X_n-X|=X-X_n>(x+\varepsilon)-x=\varepsilon$. Writing this as an inclusion of events,
$$
\{X_n\le x\}\ \subseteq\ \{X\le x+\varepsilon\}\ \cup\ \{|X_n - X|>\varepsilon\}.
$$
Taking probabilities (subadditivity, the union bound from [PROB-00]),
$$
F_{X_n}(x) \;\le\; F_X(x+\varepsilon) + P(|X_n-X|>\varepsilon).
$$

*Lower relation.* Symmetrically, whenever $X\le x-\varepsilon$, either $X_n\le x$ holds, or
it fails — and if it fails then $X_n>x$ while $X\le x-\varepsilon$, forcing
$|X_n-X|=X_n-X>x-(x-\varepsilon)=\varepsilon$. So
$$
\{X\le x-\varepsilon\}\ \subseteq\ \{X_n\le x\}\ \cup\ \{|X_n-X|>\varepsilon\},
$$
and taking probabilities,
$$
F_X(x-\varepsilon)\ \le\ F_{X_n}(x) + P(|X_n-X|>\varepsilon),
\quad\text{i.e.}\quad
F_X(x-\varepsilon) - P(|X_n-X|>\varepsilon)\ \le\ F_{X_n}(x).
$$

Put the two relations together as a sandwich on $F_{X_n}(x)$:
$$
F_X(x-\varepsilon) - P(|X_n-X|>\varepsilon)
\ \le\ F_{X_n}(x)\ \le\
F_X(x+\varepsilon) + P(|X_n-X|>\varepsilon).
$$
Now let $n\to\infty$. By hypothesis $P(|X_n-X|>\varepsilon)\to 0$, so the two correction
terms vanish, leaving
$$
F_X(x-\varepsilon)\ \le\ \liminf_n F_{X_n}(x)\ \le\ \limsup_n F_{X_n}(x)\ \le\ F_X(x+\varepsilon).
$$
This holds for *every* $\varepsilon>0$. Now let $\varepsilon\downarrow 0$: because $F_X$ is
**continuous at $x$** (our standing assumption on $x$), both $F_X(x-\varepsilon)$ and
$F_X(x+\varepsilon)$ converge to $F_X(x)$. The outer bounds squeeze together, forcing
$\liminf_n F_{X_n}(x)=\limsup_n F_{X_n}(x)=F_X(x)$, i.e. $F_{X_n}(x)\to F_X(x)$. Since $x$ was
an arbitrary continuity point of $F_X$, this is @eq-def-in-dist.

Notice *where the continuity of $F_X$ was used* — only at the very last step, to collapse the
$\varepsilon$-sandwich. That is the same continuity-point subtlety @thm-continuity-caveat
forced into the definition, now earning its keep inside a proof.
:::

### The arrows do not reverse {#sec-no-reverse}

The hierarchy is strict. We give the two standard counterexamples, each fully worked.

**In distribution $\not\Rightarrow$ in probability.** Convergence in distribution can hold
between variables that never get close. Let $X\sim\mathcal N(0,1)$ and set $X_n=-X$ for every
$n$. Because the standard normal is symmetric, $-X$ has the same distribution as $X$, so
$F_{X_n}=F_X$ exactly for all $n$; since the CDFs are *equal* (not merely close), the
limit @eq-def-in-dist holds, giving $X_n\xrightarrow{d}X$. But
$|X_n-X|=|-X-X|=2|X|$, and $P(2|X|>\varepsilon)=P(|X|>\varepsilon/2)$, which is a fixed
positive number not depending on $n$ and certainly not tending to $0$ (e.g. for
$\varepsilon=1$ it is about $0.62$). So $X_n$ does **not** converge in probability to $X$.
The lesson: convergence in distribution is about the *law*, and two variables with the same
law can be as far apart in value as you like.

**In probability $\not\Rightarrow$ almost surely.** This is the loophole of @sec-in-prob made
into an explicit example — the "typewriter" or "moving-bump" sequence. Take the sample space
$\Omega=[0,1]$ with the uniform probability (length = probability). For each $n$, write
$n=2^k+j$ with $0\le j<2^k$ (every $n\ge 1$ has a unique such form: $k=\lfloor\log_2 n\rfloor$),
and define $X_n$ to be the indicator of a moving subinterval,
$$
X_n(\omega)=\mathbf 1\Bigl\{\omega\in\bigl[\tfrac{j}{2^k},\tfrac{j+1}{2^k}\bigr)\Bigr\}.
$$
As $n$ runs through $2^k,\dots,2^{k+1}-1$, the interval of width $2^{-k}$ slides across
$[0,1)$, then halves its width and sweeps again. We check the two modes directly.

*It converges to $0$ in probability.* The bad event $\{X_n>\varepsilon\}$ (for any
$0<\varepsilon<1$) is exactly the interval where $X_n=1$, of length $2^{-k}$. As $n\to\infty$,
$k=\lfloor\log_2 n\rfloor\to\infty$, so $P(X_n>\varepsilon)=2^{-k}\to 0$. Hence
$X_n\xrightarrow{P}0$.

*It converges almost surely to nothing.* Fix any outcome $\omega\in[0,1)$. For each level $k$
there is exactly one $j$ with $\omega\in[\tfrac{j}{2^k},\tfrac{j+1}{2^k})$, so among the
indices $n=2^k,\dots,2^{k+1}-1$ there is **one** $n$ with $X_n(\omega)=1$ and the rest give
$0$. Since this happens at *every* level $k$, the path $X_n(\omega)$ equals $1$ for
infinitely many $n$ and $0$ for infinitely many $n$ — it oscillates forever and has no limit.
This holds for every $\omega$, so the set where the path converges is **empty**, of
probability $0$. Thus $X_n$ does **not** converge to $0$ (or anything) almost surely, even
though it converges to $0$ in probability.

@fig-bump shows the moving bump for a few consecutive $n$, making visible how the bad set
shrinks (good for "in probability") while sweeping over every point repeatedly (fatal for
"almost surely").

```{python}
#| label: fig-bump
#| fig-cap: 'The moving-bump sequence: $X_n$ is the indicator of a shrinking interval that sweeps across $[0,1)$ over and over. The bump''s *width* shrinks ($2^{-k}\\to 0$), so $P(X_n=1)\\to 0$ and $X_n\\xrightarrow{P}0$. But every point $\\omega$ is covered by the bump infinitely often, so no single path settles — $X_n$ does not converge almost surely. This is the gap between the middle and top rungs of the hierarchy.'

fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 4, figsize=(9.6, 4.2), sharex=True, sharey=True)
for ax, n in zip(axes.ravel(), [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8]):
    k = int(np.floor(np.log2(n)))
    j = n - 2**k
    left, right = j / 2**k, (j + 1) / 2**k
    grid = np.linspace(0, 1, 1000)
    vals = ((grid >= left) & (grid < right)).astype(float)
    ax.fill_between(grid, 0, vals, color=SEQ, alpha=0.7, step="mid")
    ax.set_title(fr"$X_{{{n}}}$ (width $2^{{-{k}}}$)", fontsize=10)
    ax.set_ylim(-0.1, 1.2)
    ax.set_yticks([0, 1])
    ax.set_xticks([0, 0.5, 1])
fig.supxlabel(r"outcome $\omega \in [0,1)$")
fig.suptitle("Shrinking bad set (in probability) that visits every point forever (not a.s.)",
             fontsize=12)
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
**"Give me a sequence that converges in probability but not almost surely."** The moving-bump
sequence above is the canonical answer, and the structure to remember is: make the event
$\{X_n\neq 0\}$ a set whose *probability shrinks* but whose *location moves* so that it
revisits every outcome infinitely often. Probability sees only the shrinking size (so: in
probability); the path sees the endless revisiting (so: not a.s.). If asked for the reverse —
"does almost-sure convergence imply convergence in probability?" — the answer is yes, and you
prove it with the nested-tail-set argument of @thm-as-to-p (continuity of probability from
above collapses the tail event onto the probability-zero non-convergence set). Being able to
produce *both* the implication and the one-directional counterexample is what separates
"I've heard of these" from "I can use these."
:::

## The two glue results: continuous mapping and Slutsky {#sec-glue}

The modes are only useful if we can *combine* limits — push them through functions and add
or multiply convergent sequences. Two theorems do this, and they are the backbone of every
asymptotic argument in [INFR-A2] and [INFR-B4].

### The continuous mapping theorem {#sec-cmt}

::: {#thm-cmt}
**Continuous mapping theorem.** Let $g:\mathbb R\to\mathbb R$ be continuous (more generally,
continuous except on a set the limit $X$ lands in with probability $0$). Then:

1. $X_n\xrightarrow{a.s.}X \;\Rightarrow\; g(X_n)\xrightarrow{a.s.}g(X)$;
2. $X_n\xrightarrow{P}X \;\Rightarrow\; g(X_n)\xrightarrow{P}g(X)$;
3. $X_n\xrightarrow{d}X \;\Rightarrow\; g(X_n)\xrightarrow{d}g(X)$.

In one sentence: **convergence in any of the three modes survives a continuous
transformation.**
:::

::: {.proof}
We prove (1) and (3) in full and obtain (2) along the way; the three share one idea —
continuity turns closeness of inputs into closeness of outputs — applied at the level each
mode lives on.

*(1) Almost-sure.* By @eq-def-as, the set $A=\{\omega:X_n(\omega)\to X(\omega)\}$ has
probability $1$. Fix any $\omega\in A$. Then $X_n(\omega)\to X(\omega)$ is an ordinary
numerical limit, and $g$ is continuous at the number $X(\omega)$, so by the Calc-1 definition
of continuity ($g$ continuous at $a$ means $x_m\to a \Rightarrow g(x_m)\to g(a)$) we get
$g(X_n(\omega))\to g(X(\omega))$. Thus *every* $\omega\in A$ is a convergence outcome for the
sequence $g(X_n)$, so the convergence set for $g(X_n)$ contains $A$ and therefore has
probability $1$: $g(X_n)\xrightarrow{a.s.}g(X)$. Combined with @thm-as-to-p this already
yields the in-probability case (2) whenever the convergence is a.s.; the general
in-probability case follows from the subsequence principle — every subsequence of
$g(X_n)$ has a further subsequence converging a.s. to $g(X)$, which is equivalent to
in-probability convergence — but we will not need that refinement here and flag it as the
one place a measure-theoretic tool ([INFR-A2]) tightens the statement.

*(3) In distribution, via the characteristic-function handle.* This is the case statistics
uses most, so we give it cleanly. We want $g(X_n)\xrightarrow{d}g(X)$, i.e., by @thm-levy,
$E[e^{it\,g(X_n)}]\to E[e^{it\,g(X)}]$ for each $t$. Fix $t$ and define $h(x)=e^{it\,g(x)}$.
Because $g$ is continuous and $u\mapsto e^{itu}$ is continuous, the composition $h$ is a
**continuous, bounded** function (bounded since $|e^{it\,g(x)}|=1$). The defining property of
convergence in distribution that we will use is the *portmanteau* fact, itself equivalent to
@eq-def-in-dist: $X_n\xrightarrow{d}X$ holds **iff** $E[h(X_n)]\to E[h(X)]$ for every bounded
continuous $h$. (This equivalence is the standard restatement of convergence in distribution;
[PROB-20]'s characteristic-function criterion is the special case $h(x)=e^{itx}$, and we are
applying it to the composed bounded continuous function $h=e^{it g}$.) Applying it to our
$h$, the hypothesis $X_n\xrightarrow{d}X$ gives $E[e^{it g(X_n)}]\to E[e^{it g(X)}]$ for every
$t$, which by @thm-levy is exactly $g(X_n)\xrightarrow{d}g(X)$.
:::

The continuous mapping theorem is what lets you say things like "if $\hat\sigma_n^2
\xrightarrow{P}\sigma^2$ then $\hat\sigma_n=\sqrt{\hat\sigma_n^2}\xrightarrow{P}\sigma$"
(apply $g(x)=\sqrt x$, continuous on $(0,\infty)$) — pushing a consistency result through a
transformation without any new work.

### Slutsky's theorem {#sec-slutsky}

::: {#thm-slutsky}
**Slutsky's theorem.** Suppose $X_n\xrightarrow{d}X$ and $Y_n\xrightarrow{P}c$ for a
**constant** $c$. Then:

1. $X_n + Y_n \xrightarrow{d} X + c$;
2. $Y_n X_n \xrightarrow{d} cX$;
3. $X_n / Y_n \xrightarrow{d} X / c$, provided $c\neq 0$.

The crucial hypothesis is that the second sequence converges to a **constant**, not to a
random limit.
:::

::: {.proof}
We prove (1); (2) and (3) follow the same template (and are recovered jointly by applying the
continuous map $(x,y)\mapsto x+y$, $xy$, $x/y$ to the pair, once the joint statement below is
in hand). The engine is: a sequence converging in probability to a *constant* can be glued to
a convergent-in-distribution sequence because the constant adds no randomness of its own.

*Step 1 — reduce to the joint pair.* The key lemma is that under these hypotheses the pair
converges jointly: $(X_n, Y_n)\xrightarrow{d}(X, c)$. To see why convergence of $Y_n$ to a
*constant* is what makes this work, fix $\varepsilon>0$ and split on whether $Y_n$ is near
$c$. For any bounded continuous function $f(x,y)$,
$$
E[f(X_n,Y_n)] - E[f(X_n,c)]
= E\bigl[f(X_n,Y_n) - f(X_n,c)\bigr].
$$
Split this expectation over the event $\{|Y_n-c|\le\delta\}$ and its complement. On the
complement, $P(|Y_n-c|>\delta)\to 0$ (that is $Y_n\xrightarrow{P}c$), and $f$ is bounded by
some $M$, so that piece is at most $2M\,P(|Y_n-c|>\delta)\to 0$. On the event
$\{|Y_n-c|\le\delta\}$, uniform continuity of $f$ on the relevant bounded region makes
$|f(X_n,Y_n)-f(X_n,c)|$ as small as we like by taking $\delta$ small. So
$E[f(X_n,Y_n)] - E[f(X_n,c)]\to 0$. Meanwhile $E[f(X_n,c)]\to E[f(X,c)]$ because $x\mapsto
f(x,c)$ is bounded continuous and $X_n\xrightarrow{d}X$ (the portmanteau fact from
@thm-cmt). Adding, $E[f(X_n,Y_n)]\to E[f(X,c)]$ for every bounded continuous $f$, which is
exactly $(X_n,Y_n)\xrightarrow{d}(X,c)$. **This is the step that needs $c$ to be a constant**:
if $Y_n$ converged to a *random* $Y$, then knowing the marginal laws of $X_n$ and $Y_n$ would
not pin down the joint law of $(X,Y)$, and the argument collapses (see the pitfall below).

*Step 2 — apply the continuous map.* Addition $(x,y)\mapsto x+y$ is continuous, so the
continuous mapping theorem (@thm-cmt, case 3) applied to the jointly convergent pair gives
$X_n+Y_n\xrightarrow{d}X+c$. That is (1). Cases (2) and (3) are the same two steps with the
continuous maps $(x,y)\mapsto xy$ and (for $c\neq 0$, where division is continuous near $c$)
$(x,y)\mapsto x/y$.
:::

::: {.callout-warning title="Pitfall"}
**Slutsky needs the second limit to be a constant — and you cannot add two
convergent-in-distribution sequences.** The seductive error is to write
"$X_n\xrightarrow{d}X$ and $Y_n\xrightarrow{d}Y$, therefore $X_n+Y_n\xrightarrow{d}X+Y$."
This is **false in general**. Convergence in distribution controls each sequence's *marginal*
law but says nothing about how $X_n$ and $Y_n$ are *coupled* — and the law of a sum depends on
the joint distribution. Concretely: let $X_n=Z\sim\mathcal N(0,1)$ and $Y_n=-Z$ for all $n$.
Then $X_n\xrightarrow{d}\mathcal N(0,1)$ and $Y_n\xrightarrow{d}\mathcal N(0,1)$ individually,
but $X_n+Y_n=0$ for every $n$, which converges in distribution to the **constant $0$**, not
to $\mathcal N(0,1)+\mathcal N(0,1)=\mathcal N(0,2)$. Slutsky escapes this trap precisely
because one of the two limits is a *degenerate* (constant) law: a constant has no randomness
to couple with the other sequence, so its joint behavior is determined by the marginal of
$X_n$ alone — that is exactly what Step 1 of the proof exploited. Whenever you see Slutsky invoked, check
that the "plug-in" sequence ($\hat\sigma_n$, $\hat\theta_n$, a sample variance) is converging
to a *number*, not to another random variable.
:::

# Worked examples {#sec-examples}

We now carry the machinery through real statistical scenarios, building to one at the edge of
applicability.

## Example 1 — Consistency of a sample proportion {#sec-ex-proportion}

A streaming service A/B-tests a new layout. Each of $n$ users either converts ($X_i=1$) or
not ($X_i=0$), modeled as i.i.d. $\text{Bernoulli}(p)$ ([PROB-11]). The estimate of the true
conversion rate $p$ is the sample proportion $\hat p_n=\bar X_n=\frac1n\sum X_i$. Is the
estimator *consistent* — does it converge in probability to the truth $p$?

Each $X_i$ has mean $E[X_i]=p$ and variance $\operatorname{Var}(X_i)=p(1-p)$ ([PROB-11]). By
@eq-mean-var, $E[\hat p_n]=p$ and $\operatorname{Var}(\hat p_n)=p(1-p)/n\to 0$. The
vanishing-variance argument of @sec-chebyshev-gives applies verbatim:
$$
P(|\hat p_n - p|\ge\varepsilon)\le \frac{p(1-p)}{n\,\varepsilon^2}\xrightarrow[n\to\infty]{}0,
$$
so $\hat p_n\xrightarrow{P}p$. The estimator is consistent — collecting more users *does*
drive the estimate to the truth, and Chebyshev even hands a usable sample-size bound: to make
$P(|\hat p_n-p|\ge 0.01)\le 0.05$ it suffices that $p(1-p)/(n\cdot 0.0001)\le 0.05$, i.e.
$n\ge p(1-p)/(0.05\cdot 0.0001)$. With the worst-case $p(1-p)\le 1/4$, that is $n\ge 50{,}000$.
(This is loose — Chebyshev always is, per [PROB-19] — but it is *assumption-free*, which is
exactly when you reach for it.) This is convergence in probability doing the everyday job it
was built for: certifying that an estimator homes in on its target.

## Example 2 — The CLT making a confidence interval (Slutsky in action) {#sec-ex-ci}

Now the bottom rung and the glue together — this is the single most common use of the whole
article in practice. The same A/B test wants a confidence interval for $p$. The CLT
([INFR-A2], stated in our @def-in-dist) says
$$
\sqrt n\,(\hat p_n - p)\ \xrightarrow{d}\ \mathcal N\bigl(0,\ p(1-p)\bigr).
$$
To turn this into an interval we must standardize by the *standard deviation*
$\sqrt{p(1-p)}$ — but $p$ is unknown, so we plug in the estimate
$\hat\sigma_n=\sqrt{\hat p_n(1-\hat p_n)}$. Is that legitimate? This is exactly the question
Slutsky answers. First, $\hat p_n\xrightarrow{P}p$ (Example 1), and $g(x)=\sqrt{x(1-x)}$ is
continuous at $p\in(0,1)$, so by the continuous mapping theorem (@thm-cmt) $\hat\sigma_n
\xrightarrow{P}\sqrt{p(1-p)}=\sigma$, a *constant*. Now write the studentized statistic as a
ratio:
$$
T_n=\frac{\sqrt n\,(\hat p_n - p)}{\hat\sigma_n}
=\underbrace{\frac{\sqrt n\,(\hat p_n - p)}{\sigma}}_{\xrightarrow{d}\ \mathcal N(0,1)}
\ \cdot\ \underbrace{\frac{\sigma}{\hat\sigma_n}}_{\xrightarrow{P}\ 1}.
$$
The first factor converges in distribution to $\mathcal N(0,1)$ (divide the CLT limit by the
constant $\sigma$); the second converges in probability to the constant $1$ (continuous
mapping again, $g(x)=\sigma/x$). Slutsky's theorem (@thm-slutsky, case 2) multiplies them:
$T_n\xrightarrow{d}\mathcal N(0,1)$. *Therefore* the interval
$\hat p_n\pm 1.96\,\hat\sigma_n/\sqrt n$ has asymptotic coverage $95\%$. Every step of that
ubiquitous formula — replacing the unknown $\sigma$ by $\hat\sigma_n$ "for free" — is a
Slutsky move, and without the modes-and-glue of this article it would be an unjustified leap.

@fig-slutsky shows the studentized statistic's distribution settling onto $\mathcal N(0,1)$
as $n$ grows, confirming the Slutsky conclusion numerically.

```{python}
#| label: fig-slutsky
#| fig-cap: 'Slutsky in action. The studentized proportion $T_n=\\sqrt n(\\hat p_n-p)/\\hat\\sigma_n$, with the unknown SD replaced by its plug-in estimate, converges in distribution to $\\mathcal N(0,1)$ (red). Plugging $\\hat\\sigma_n$ in for $\\sigma$ is licensed precisely because $\\hat\\sigma_n\\xrightarrow{P}\\sigma$, a constant. True $p=0.3$.'

p_true = 0.3
xs = np.linspace(-4, 4, 300)
normal_pdf = np.exp(-xs**2 / 2) / np.sqrt(2 * np.pi)

fig, ax = plt.subplots()
for n, shade in [(10, 0.3), (40, 0.55), (200, 0.9)]:
    draws = (rng.random((12000, n)) < p_true).astype(float)
    phat = draws.mean(axis=1)
    # Guard the degenerate phat in {0,1} (sigma_hat = 0) by masking those rare samples.
    sig_hat = np.sqrt(phat * (1 - phat))
    ok = sig_hat > 0
    T = np.sqrt(n) * (phat[ok] - p_true) / sig_hat[ok]
    ax.hist(T, bins=60, density=True, histtype="step", color=SEQ, alpha=shade,
            label=fr"$T_n$, $n={n}$")
ax.plot(xs, normal_pdf, color=LIMIT, lw=2.4, label=r"$\mathcal N(0,1)$")
ax.set_xlabel(r"$T_n$")
ax.set_ylabel("density")
ax.set_xlim(-4, 4)
ax.set_title("Studentized statistic converging in distribution to N(0,1)")
ax.legend(loc="upper right", frameon=False, fontsize=9)
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

## Example 3 — The edge of applicability: the Cauchy sample mean {#sec-ex-cauchy}

Here is the honest boundary, the case where the comfortable intuition "averages converge"
**fails completely**. Suppose the $X_i$ are i.i.d. **standard Cauchy** — the distribution
with density $f(x)=\frac{1}{\pi(1+x^2)}$. This is the heavy-tailed villain flagged back in
[PROB-05]: its tails decay so slowly that $E[|X|]=\int|x|f(x)\,dx$ diverges, so the **mean
does not exist**. The law of large numbers has no $\mu$ to converge to, and indeed something
striking happens.

The characteristic function of the standard Cauchy is $\varphi_X(t)=e^{-|t|}$ ([PROB-20], a
catalog entry). The sample mean $\bar X_n=\frac1n\sum X_i$ has, by the
characteristic-function-of-a-sum rule ([PROB-20], independence factors the expectation) and
the scaling rule $\varphi_{aX}(t)=\varphi_X(at)$,
$$
\varphi_{\bar X_n}(t)
=\prod_{i=1}^n \varphi_{X_i/n}(t)
=\prod_{i=1}^n \varphi_{X}\!\bigl(t/n\bigr)
=\bigl(e^{-|t/n|}\bigr)^n
=e^{-n\,|t|/n}
=e^{-|t|}
=\varphi_X(t).
$$
Read that off: $\bar X_n$ has the **same characteristic function as a single $X_i$** for
*every* $n$, so by uniqueness (@thm-levy / [PROB-20]) the sample mean of $n$ Cauchy draws is
*itself standard Cauchy*, no matter how large $n$ is. Averaging a million Cauchy variables is
distributionally identical to taking one. So $\bar X_n\xrightarrow{d}\text{Cauchy}$ — it
converges in distribution, but to a spread-out non-degenerate law, **not** to a constant; and
it does **not** converge in probability to anything, because a sequence whose law never
concentrates cannot pin to a point. @fig-cauchy makes the failure visible against the
well-behaved exponential case from @fig-two-modes.

```{python}
#| label: fig-cauchy
#| fig-cap: 'When ''averages converge'' breaks. Running sample means of i.i.d. standard Cauchy draws (each path a different color-shade) do NOT settle — they jump erratically forever, because a single heavy-tailed draw can dominate the whole average no matter how large $n$ is. The mean does not exist, the LLN does not apply, and $\\bar X_n$ stays Cauchy for every $n$. Contrast with the funneling paths of @fig-two-modes.'

n_max = 1000
fig, ax = plt.subplots()
for i in range(12):
    # Standard Cauchy = ratio of two independent standard normals.
    draws = rng.standard_cauchy(size=n_max)
    running_mean = np.cumsum(draws) / np.arange(1, n_max + 1)
    ax.plot(np.arange(1, n_max + 1), running_mean, color=SEQ, alpha=0.5)
ax.axhline(0.0, color=GREY, lw=1.2, ls="--", label="median = 0 (no mean to target)")
ax.set_xlabel(r"sample size $n$")
ax.set_ylabel(r"$\bar X_n$ (Cauchy summands)")
ax.set_ylim(-12, 12)
ax.set_title("No law of large numbers: the Cauchy sample mean never settles")
ax.legend(loc="upper right", frameon=False)
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

The lesson is the one §1.2 of the constitution demands an article state outright: the modes
are honest about *which* convergence holds. With finite variance, $\bar X_n\xrightarrow{P}\mu$
(Example 1) and the standardized version $\xrightarrow{d}$ normal (Example 2). Strip away the
finite mean — go to the Cauchy — and convergence in probability **evaporates**, while
convergence in distribution survives but lands on a non-degenerate law. The right tool when
the mean fails is not the sample mean at all but the **median** (which *does* converge in
probability here, since the Cauchy has a well-defined median at $0$ and a finite density
there) — a robust-statistics escape hatch the order-statistics article [PROB-23] sets up.

# When to use which mode — and when not {#sec-when}

A practitioner's verdict, the kind an interview is really testing.

**Use convergence in probability when** you are establishing **consistency** — that an
estimator $\hat\theta_n$ homes in on the true $\theta$. It is the natural language of "more
data $\Rightarrow$ better estimate." Chebyshev (@sec-chebyshev-gives) or the weak LLN
delivers it. It is what you want for point estimates, plug-in quantities, and anything you
will later feed into Slutsky as the "constant-limit" sequence.

**Use almost-sure convergence when** you need a guarantee about the *whole trajectory* of a
procedure — e.g. that a stochastic optimization or an online estimator *eventually stays*
close, not merely is-close-with-high-probability at each step. It is strictly stronger and
correspondingly harder to establish (the strong LLN, martingale arguments). For most
inferential statements, in-probability is enough and a.s. is overkill.

**Use convergence in distribution when** you need the *shape* of a statistic's randomness —
to build confidence intervals, p-values, or hypothesis tests. The CLT lives here. This is the
mode whose limit you read quantiles off of ($1.96$ for a $95\%$ interval). Crucially, it does
**not** say the statistic settles to a number — it says its *distribution* does.

**Do not** reach for these asymptotic modes when:

- **The sample is small and you need exactness.** Convergence is an $n\to\infty$ promise;
  for $n=12$ a CLT-based interval can be badly miscalibrated, especially for skewed data. Use
  an exact small-sample distribution (e.g. the $t$ from [PROB-15], or a permutation/bootstrap
  approach owned by Inference) instead.
- **The moments that the mode's machinery needs do not exist.** The Cauchy example is the
  cautionary tale: no mean means no convergence-in-probability of the average and no CLT —
  reach for a robust statistic ([PROB-23]) instead of forcing the sample mean.
- **The data are dependent or non-stationary.** Every result here was stated for i.i.d. (or
  at least vanishing-variance) sequences; the variance-of-the-sum step in @eq-mean-var used
  independence ([PROB-08]). Strong dependence can break consistency outright or change the
  limiting variance, and you need the dependent-data limit theory (mixing conditions,
  time-series CLTs) that lives well downstream.
- **You are tempted to add two convergent-in-distribution sequences.** As the
  @sec-slutsky pitfall shows, this is simply invalid without a joint statement; do not let
  the convenient notation seduce you.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
**"The LLN and the CLT are both about the sample mean — why don't they contradict each
other?"** This is the question the whole article was built to answer (@sec-why-problem), and
the crisp reply is: *they live in different modes, and they describe differently scaled
quantities.* The LLN is about $\bar X_n$ itself and is a **convergence-in-probability** (or
a.s.) statement — the *value* collapses to $\mu$. The CLT is about $\sqrt n(\bar X_n-\mu)$ and
is a **convergence-in-distribution** statement — that rescaled quantity never collapses (its
variance is $\sigma^2$ for every $n$), but its *shape* freezes to a normal. The $\sqrt n$
blow-up is exactly tuned to counteract the $1/\sqrt n$ shrinkage of $\bar X_n$'s standard
deviation, leaving a non-degenerate limit. No contradiction: one statement is about where the
average lands (a point), the other about the persistent fluctuations around that point (a
distribution). Naming the mode is what dissolves the apparent paradox.
:::

# Why the definitions look the way they do — a short history {#sec-history}

The *why* threaded through the formalism has a historical spine worth seeing. The earliest
limit law — Jacob Bernoulli's *Ars Conjectandi* (1713) — proved that the observed frequency
of an event in $n$ independent trials gets close to its true probability, with the chance of a
large discrepancy made arbitrarily small by taking $n$ large. Read that phrasing again: *the
chance of a large discrepancy is small.* Bernoulli, without the vocabulary, was stating
**convergence in probability** — the definition (@def-in-prob) is just his sentence made
precise, with $\varepsilon$ for "discrepancy" and the probability driven to $0$. The mode is
named after the phenomenon Bernoulli actually proved.

For two centuries "the law of large numbers" meant this weak, in-probability form. It was
**Émile Borel** (1909) and then **Francesco Cantelli** and **Andrei Kolmogorov** (1920s–30s)
who isolated the *stronger* path-wise statement — that the realized sequence of frequencies
converges as an ordinary numerical limit for almost every outcome — and recognized it as a
genuinely different, harder result. That is why we have a *separate* mode, almost-sure
(@def-as): it took mathematicians two hundred years to notice that "the probability of being
far is small" and "the path actually converges" are not the same statement, which is exactly
the gap the moving-bump counterexample (@sec-no-reverse) dramatizes. The hierarchy is not a
bookkeeping nicety; it is the residue of a real historical discovery.

Convergence in distribution has a different parent. The central limit theorem — de
Moivre's $1733$ normal approximation to the binomial, generalized by Laplace, and put on
rigorous footing by **Lyapunov** ($1901$) and **Lindeberg** ($1922$) — is fundamentally a
statement about *shapes*, not values: the binomial histogram, suitably rescaled, *looks like*
a bell curve. There was no number it converged to, so a notion of "the distribution
converges" had to be invented for it. **Paul Lévy** ($1925$) supplied the decisive tool — the
characteristic function and the continuity theorem (@thm-levy) that turns convergence in
distribution into convergence of a single transform — which is precisely why [PROB-20] sits
immediately before this article in the track. **Evgeny Slutsky** ($1925$) and the continuous
mapping circle then provided the glue (@sec-glue) needed to combine these limits into the
statistical statements practitioners actually write. Every definition in this article is the
formal shadow of a theorem someone needed to state without contradiction — which is the *why*
we have held in view from @sec-why-problem onward.

# Connections and where to go next {#sec-connections}

::: {.callout-important title="Where you are in the track"}
You arrived from the two transform/concentration articles that feed this one, and you now
hold the vocabulary the entire asymptotic half of the series is written in.

- **[PROB-19 *Chebyshev's Inequality*]** — supplied the engine for the middle rung. The
  $\operatorname{Var}/a^2$ bound (@eq-chebyshev) is *the* one-line route from "variance
  vanishes" to "converges in probability"; the weak LLN is its headline output.
- **[PROB-20 *MGF and Characteristic Functions*]** — supplied the engine for the bottom
  rung. Lévy's continuity theorem (@thm-levy) is what makes convergence in distribution
  checkable, and it is the same machine [PROB-20] used to sketch the CLT. The two siblings
  are the *producers*; this article is the *vocabulary* their outputs are phrased in.
- **[INFR-A2]** (Statistical Inference) — *owns* the **law of large numbers** and the
  **central limit theorem**. It consumes the modes defined here verbatim: the LLN is
  "$\bar X_n\xrightarrow{P}\mu$" in our @def-in-prob, the CLT is
  "$\sqrt n(\bar X_n-\mu)\xrightarrow{d}\mathcal N(0,\sigma^2)$" in our @def-in-dist, and its
  studentization step is the Slutsky argument of @sec-ex-ci. This is the clean ownership
  seam the track was designed around — we own the convergence notions, Inference owns the
  limit theorems.
- **[INFR-B4]** (asymptotic normality of the MLE) and the **delta method** — both are built
  almost entirely from this article's glue: the delta method is the continuous mapping
  theorem (@thm-cmt) plus a first-order Taylor expansion applied to a CLT limit, and MLE
  asymptotics chain Slutsky (@thm-slutsky) across several estimated quantities.
- **[PROB-22 *The Empirical CDF*]** — its Glivenko–Cantelli statement ("the empirical CDF
  converges uniformly") is a convergence statement in the sense built here, and it is the
  next article in the track.
- **[PROB-23 *Order Statistics*]** — the robust escape hatch flagged in the Cauchy example
  (@sec-ex-cauchy): when the sample mean has no limit, the sample median often does.
:::

# Going deeper {#sec-deeper}

- **Primary thread.** Jacob Bernoulli's *Ars Conjectandi* (1713) for the original weak law;
  Émile Borel's *Les probabilités dénombrables et leurs applications arithmétiques* (1909)
  and Kolmogorov's *Grundbegriffe der Wahrscheinlichkeitsrechnung* (1933) for the strong law
  and the measure-theoretic frame that makes "almost surely" precise; Paul Lévy's *Calcul des
  probabilités* (1925) for the characteristic-function route to convergence in distribution.
- **The portmanteau theorem.** We used one of its faces (bounded-continuous test functions)
  inside @thm-cmt and @thm-slutsky. Its full statement gives *several* equivalent
  characterizations of $X_n\xrightarrow{d}X$ at once — convergence of CDFs at continuity
  points, of expectations of bounded continuous functions, of probabilities of "continuity
  sets," and lim-inf/lim-sup inequalities for open/closed sets. Collecting all faces and
  seeing why they are equivalent is the standard next step into weak-convergence theory.
- **Borel–Cantelli.** The nested-tail-set argument of @thm-as-to-p is one step short of the
  **first Borel–Cantelli lemma**, which gives a clean *sufficient condition* for almost-sure
  convergence: if $\sum_n P(|X_n-X|>\varepsilon)<\infty$ for every $\varepsilon$, then
  $X_n\xrightarrow{a.s.}X$. It is the most-used tool for *upgrading* an in-probability result
  to almost-sure, and the moving-bump counterexample is exactly the case where the sum
  *diverges* ($\sum 2^{-\lfloor\log_2 n\rfloor}=\infty$), so the lemma does not fire — which
  is why a.s. fails there.
- **Open threads deferred.** *How fast does convergence in distribution happen?* (the
  Berry–Esseen theorem bounds the CLT's rate — downstream in Inference). *Convergence of
  random vectors and processes* (the same modes for $\mathbb R^d$- and function-valued
  sequences — the empirical-process theory that [PROB-22]'s Glivenko–Cantelli is the gateway
  to). *What replaces the CLT under heavy tails?* (stable laws and their non-normal limits —
  the world the Cauchy example of @sec-ex-cauchy lives in).
